entries.title: Dashboard overview: Tasks, Event log, Issue activity
entries.body:
# Dashboard - the control panel for Burp's automation

**Dashboard** is the first screen that greets you when you launch Burp, and at the same time the control center for all of the project's automation. Here you can see what Burp is doing right now: which tasks are running, what they found, what events happened. In short, **Dashboard** answers the question "what is Burp busy with right now and what came of it".

The screen is split into several panels. The layout drifts a little from version to version, but there are usually four meaningful blocks:

- **Tasks** - a list of automated tasks (scans, crawls, live tasks, sometimes Intruder attacks).
- **Event log** - an event journal: what started and when, what errors and warnings there were.
- **Issue activity** (Pro) - a real-time feed of scanner findings.
- **Advisory / detail panel** - a description of the selected finding or task, at the bottom/side.

> Important about editions: the heavy part of **Dashboard** is the scanner. **New scan**, **New live task**, **Issue activity**, and automatic vulnerability discovery are available only in **Professional**. In **Community Edition** you will see the screen itself, the **Event log**, the memory indicators, and you will be able to run live tasks in a limited form, but there will be no full-fledged scanning.

## The Tasks panel

**Tasks** is the queue of everything Burp runs automatically. Each task row shows its type, status, progress, and brief statistics (how many requests were sent, how many locations were found, how many issues were discovered).

The task types that land here:

- **Crawl** (Pro) - crawling the application to build a site map (**Target > Site map**), without active checks.
- **Crawl and audit** / **Full scan** (Pro) - a crawl plus an active audit, that is, full-fledged vulnerability discovery.
- **Live tasks** (Live passive crawl & audit) - passively analyze the traffic of your manual browsing. By default a live task is already enabled in a new project that quietly analyzes everything you browse.
- **Intruder attacks** - running brute-forces are also displayed as tasks.

What you can do with tasks:

- **Start a new one.** The **New scan** and **New live task** buttons at the top of the panel open the scan/live-task configuration wizard.
- **Pause/resume.** The play/pause button in the header of the whole list pauses/resumes ALL tasks at once; the same button by a specific task's header controls only it. By default, when you reopen a saved project, tasks start paused - this is intentional, so a scan does not unexpectedly tear off into the background.
- **Capturing for live tasks.** Live tasks have a separate **Capturing** toggle - it turns the capture of new traffic on/off without touching what is already queued for analysis.
- **Open the details.** Clicking a task expands the particulars: settings, progress by phase, found issues, and also the **Logger** of that specific task (Task Logger).
- **Rename.** You can give tasks meaningful names - handy when there are several scans and you must not mix them up.

## The Event log panel

**Event log** is a chronological event journal of the whole project. Burp writes task starts, progress, warnings, and errors here. Each record contains:

- **time** - the time of the event;
- **type** - the level: **Critical**, **Error**, **Info**, or **Debug**;
- **source** - what produced the event (a task number or a tool name);
- **message** - a brief description.

Why look at the **Event log** in practice:

- **A scan "is not working".** Come here first: there will be messages like "cannot connect", "target out of scope", "got nothing but timeouts". Often the cause is visible right away.
- **Connection/TLS errors** while working with the target - Burp honestly logs them here.
- **Confirmation that a task actually started**, rather than just hanging.

> Tip: records can be filtered by level. On a noisy project, leave **Critical/Error** so the important things do not drown in **Info/Debug**.

## The Issue activity panel (Pro)

**Issue activity** is a live feed of the scanner's work: a sequential list of how new issues are found and already-found ones are updated. Each record shows:

- a sequence number (in what order it was added);
- the time of the activity;
- the action - for example **Issue found** or **Evidence added** (evidence added to an existing one);
- the issue type;
- the host and the URL path;
- the insertion point, if applicable.

Clicking a row opens a detail panel (**Advisory**) at the bottom with a description of the vulnerability, the evidence, and remediation recommendations. The convenience is that the feed is visible right during the scan - you can start triaging findings by hand without waiting for the scan to finish entirely.

## How to read this during hunting

A working view of the **Dashboard** looks like this:

```
Tasks         -> what Burp is doing right now and what % it is done
Issue activity-> what it has already found (triage right away, do not wait for the scan to end)
Event log     -> why something is going wrong (errors/timeouts/scope)
Advisory      -> details of the selected finding + remediation
```

A typical scenario: you launched **New scan** on the target -> you watch **Issue activity** as findings trickle in -> you open an issue that interested you, read the **Advisory**, send the related request to **Repeater**, and confirm it by hand -> in parallel you keep an eye on the **Event log** to see whether the scan is throwing errors.

> Pitfall: do not take the scanner at its word. **Issue activity** is candidates, not confirmed bugs. The scanner produces false positives (especially on reflected things and "possible" issues marked Tentative/Firm). Drive any finding home by hand in **Repeater** before you write it in the report.

> Pitfall: the memory and file-size indicators at the bottom are not for decoration. A large **Full scan** in a **temporary project** easily hits the memory limit. For a long engagement, set up a disk-based project and keep the scope narrow, otherwise Burp will start to slow down or crash.
